glossary.def: The authorized, defensive discipline of deliberately attacking your own model or system to find harmful, false, or policy-violating behavior before an adversary does, then feeding the findings straight back into defenses. It borrows the name from security: a red team plays the attacker so the blue team can harden what breaks. Done against a system you do not own or are not contracted to test, the same activity is an attack, not red-teaming.

Any input that elicits behavior a model's safety training was meant to prevent. Nearly every technique reduces to attacking the refusal decision in one of two ways: moving the prompt's internal representation off the learned 'harmful' direction (encoding, roleplay, translation), or drowning the harmful signal in benign context. Families include persona/roleplay, obfuscation, prefix/refusal suppression, many-shot, gradient-based suffixes, and indirect prompt injection.

An attack where the adversary never talks to the model directly: they plant text in content the model later ingests as data — a web page, PDF, calendar invite, code comment — that the model then interprets as instructions. Because current models have no reliable in-band way to distinguish trusted developer instructions from untrusted retrieved content, it is treated as a structural problem solved with a hard trust boundary and least-privilege tools, not by prompt wording. It is the most important attack for any agentic or RAG system.

A white-box jailbreak that optimizes an adversarial token suffix to maximize the probability the model's reply begins affirmatively ('Sure, here is …') instead of refusing, using gradients of the loss with respect to one-hot input tokens to rank candidate swaps. It requires access to the weights, which makes it the canonical open-model threat, and its suffixes often transfer to closed models because aligned models share a similar refusal geometry.

Building model safety like network security: independent layers (input filtering, model alignment, output filtering, system-level controls) each catch a fraction of attacks, and an attack must defeat all of them to succeed. If each layer independently misses with probability p, the bypass probability is the product of the per-layer miss rates — three mediocre filters compound to a strong system. The load-bearing assumption is independence; correlated failures collapse the product back toward the single weakest layer, so the layers must be genuinely diverse.

The fraction of attack attempts in a fixed, versioned suite that elicit prohibited behavior, as judged by a harm classifier or human review; robustness is 1 − ASR. It is only meaningful pinned to its suite version and its judge — a defense that drives a stale suite to zero can sit far higher on next month's attacks, and the judge itself has false positives and negatives. Credible safety claims state ASR as a number against a named suite plus a held-out novel-attack slice.

A small, dedicated classifier (such as Llama Guard) that screens text for disallowed content — run on the incoming prompt and any retrieved/tool content as an input filter, and on the model's completion as an output filter. The output guard is powerful because it is content-addressed: it judges what actually came out regardless of how the attacker phrased the request, catching jailbreaks that defeated alignment. Guard models live in the system layer, which is exactly where durable safety belongs for open weights.

The empirical finding from mechanistic interpretability that an aligned model's refusal behavior is often mediated by a single low-dimensional direction in activation space, rather than being diffusely distributed. This is why 'abliteration' can strip refusals from open weights without retraining, and a sharp illustration of the chapter's core caveat: safety training suppresses the expression of a capability, it does not remove the capability from the weights.

Zou, A., Wang, Z., Carlini, N. et al. (2023). Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models.
Inan, H., Upasani, K., Chi, J. et al. (2023). Llama Guard: LLM-based Input-Output Safeguarding for Human-AI Conversations.
Anil, C., Durmus, E., Sharma, M. et al. (2024). Many-shot Jailbreaking. Anthropic.
